title: About Housing Linear Regression
tagline: How a 1990 census dataset, ordinary least squares, and an interpretable baseline come together on pandyaHomeLab.

sections.title: The Problem
body: The California Housing dataset (1990 US census, 20,640 census blocks) ships with scikit-learn. Each row describes one block group — median income, house age, room counts, population, location — and the label is the **median home value** in that block, capped at $500,000.
The task is **regression**: given the eight features for an unseen block, predict its median home value. Unlike classification, the output is a continuous number, not a category.

sections.title: Approach — Why Linear Regression?
body: Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) finds the line (in 8 dimensions, the hyperplane) that minimises the sum of squared residuals between predicted and actual home values. It's the simplest regression model — and that's its superpower.

bullets.label: Why this baseline first
bullets.text: Linear regression sets the floor. Any fancier model (random forest, gradient boosting) needs to *beat* this baseline to justify its complexity.

bullets.label: Why it's interpretable
bullets.text: Each feature gets one coefficient — a bigger one means a bigger effect. You can read the model and tell a story about it. Black-box models can't do that.

bullets.label: Why scaling matters here
bullets.text: Coefficients only become comparable if features are on the same scale. We use `StandardScaler` so a 1-unit move in any feature has equal weight at training time.

bullets.label: Where it falls short
bullets.text: Real housing prices aren't linear in income or location. A 0.58 R² is honest but unspectacular — Random Forest typically reaches 0.80+ on this dataset.

sections.title: Architecture
body: Every project on pandyaHomeLab follows the **per-project 4-layer architecture** (ADR-013) — each domain is autonomous, with no cross-project imports.

sections.title: Network & Deployment
body: Lives in the ML domain alongside iris-knn and titanic-automl. Direct Docker DNS resolution to ml-mlflow for experiment logging — no external network needed.

sections.title: Read the Code Walkthrough
body: The **ML Project Walkthrough** uses iris-knn as the worked example (~1,200 lines), but the pattern applies identically here. The differences in housing-linear are the model (LinearRegression), the loader (`fetch_california_housing` instead of CSV), and the metrics (RMSE/MAE/R² instead of accuracy/F1).

bullets.label: Project anatomy
bullets.text: Annotated file tree — same 4-layer structure as iris-knn.

bullets.label: Request flow
bullets.text: A `/predict` request traced hop-by-hop from browser through all four layers.

bullets.label: Per-layer patterns
bullets.text: Factory pattern, ABC contracts, lazy init, the hyphen-vs-underscore symlink trick.

bullets.label: Python modular approach
bullets.text: Packages vs modules, absolute imports, `try: import X` for optional deps.

bullets.label: MLflow integration
bullets.text: How runs are logged, why graceful degradation matters.

sections.title: Performance
body: Trained on a 80/20 split with `random_state=42`. Linear regression on this dataset is famously *just okay* — the relationship between income/location and price is highly non-linear, especially for the cap at $500,000. We report it honestly as a baseline.

**Regression has no confusion matrix.** Instead, we look at error magnitude (RMSE, MAE) and how much variance the model explains (R²). For deeper diagnostics — residual plots, actual-vs-predicted scatter — open the run in MLflow.

metrics_detail.name: RMSE
metrics_detail.what: Root Mean Squared Error — typical prediction error in target units ($100K).
metrics_detail.why_chosen: Same units as the prediction (so 0.75 = $75K typical error), and squaring penalises big misses harder than small ones — undervaluing a $500K home should hurt more than missing by $5K.
metrics_detail.watch_out: Sensitive to outliers. One $500K-capped block predicted as $300K dominates the RMSE far more than a hundred small misses.

metrics_detail.name: MAE
metrics_detail.what: Mean Absolute Error — the average size of prediction errors, treating all errors equally.
metrics_detail.why_chosen: Easier to communicate to non-experts ("typical error is $53K") and less sensitive to outliers than RMSE. When MAE and RMSE diverge a lot, it tells you the errors are heavy-tailed.
metrics_detail.watch_out: Treats a $5K miss the same as five $1K misses. If big mistakes are *especially* costly in your domain, RMSE is the safer training objective.

metrics_detail.name: R²
metrics_detail.what: Coefficient of determination — what fraction of the variance in home prices the model explains. 0 = no better than predicting the mean. 1 = perfect.
metrics_detail.why_chosen: Unitless, lets you compare across datasets and across models. ~0.58 means the model captures about 58% of the variance — decent for OLS, but Random Forest typically reaches 0.80+ here, which tells you a lot about the non-linearity in this dataset.
metrics_detail.watch_out: R² can be misleadingly high on training data (overfitting) and misleadingly negative when the model is worse than the mean. Always report on a held-out test set.
